msg: Subject-verb agreement: use "there are" with plural nouns
title: Grammar error

msg: "Data" is plural — use "the data show" or "the data indicate"
title: Grammar note

msg: "Criteria" is plural — use "criteria are". The singular is "criterion".
title: Grammar error

msg: "Media" is plural — use "media are". The singular is "medium".
title: Grammar note

msg: GOV.UK style: treat collective nouns as singular — use "is", "was", "has" etc.
title: Subject-verb agreement

msg: "Each of" takes a singular verb — use "is", "was", or "has".
title: Subject-verb agreement

msg: "Neither" and "either" take a singular verb — use "is", "was", or "has".
title: Subject-verb agreement

msg: "One of" takes a singular verb — use "is", "was", or "has".
title: Subject-verb agreement

msg: Consider whether you need "which" (non-defining, adds info) or "that" (defining, essential info). Use a comma before "which".
title: Which vs that

msg: Possible comma splice — two independent clauses joined by just a comma. Use a full stop, semicolon, or add a conjunction like "and" or "but".
title: Comma splice

msg: Double negative detected — this may reverse your intended meaning
title: Double negative

msg: Tautology: gifts are free by definition
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: a bonus is already something extra
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: a result is already the end
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: history is already in the past
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: plans are for the future by definition
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Redundant modifier: the verb already implies completeness
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: warnings are given in advance by definition
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: innovations are new by definition
title: Redundant phrase

msg: "Each and every" is redundant — use "each" or "every"
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Tautology: fundamentals are basic by definition
title: Redundant phrase

msg: "Plan" already implies looking ahead — "ahead" is redundant
title: Redundant phrase

msg: Use "would have been" instead of "would of been"
title: Grammar error

msg: In British English, use "different from" not "different than"
title: Grammar error

msg: Use "bored with" or "bored by", not "bored of"
title: Grammar error

msg: Check: "literally" means it actually happened. Did you mean "figuratively"?
title: Word misuse

msg: The expression is "could not care less" (meaning you care the minimum amount)
title: Grammar error

msg: The expression is "for all intents and purposes"
title: Grammar error

msg: The expression is "one and the same"
title: Grammar error

msg: The expression is "piqued my interest" (piqued = stimulated)
title: Grammar error

msg: "Peek" means a quick look. "Peak" is the top of a mountain.
title: Grammar error

msg: "Whet" means to sharpen/stimulate. "Wet" means to make damp.
title: Grammar error

msg: The expression is "home in on" (like homing in). "Hone" means to sharpen.
title: Grammar error

msg: Use "carry out" or "set up" instead of "implement"
title: Use plain English

msg: Prefer "to" over "in order to" for brevity
title: Use plain English

msg: Prefer "before" over "prior to" for plain English
title: Use plain English

msg: Prefer "in line with" or "following" over "in accordance with"
title: Use plain English

msg: Prefer "regularly" or "often" over "on a regular basis"
title: Use plain English

msg: Prefer "now" over "at this point in time"
title: Use plain English

msg: Be specific — say how many instead of "a number of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Write "that is" or rephrase instead of "i.e."
title: Latin abbreviation

msg: GOV.UK style: avoid "etc" — list the items or say "for example"
title: Latin abbreviation

msg: Write "in itself", "as such", or "by itself" instead of "per se"
title: Latin abbreviation

msg: GOV.UK style: use "through" or "by" instead of "via"
title: Latin abbreviation

msg: Avoid "agenda" (unless for a meeting) — say what you mean: plan, approach
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "going forward" — say "from now on" or be specific about timing
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "land" as a verb (unless about aircraft) — try "get", "secure", "achieve"
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "one-stop shop" — describe what the service actually does
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "overarching" — try "overall" or just remove it
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "portal" — use "website" or "service" or the service name
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "transform" — be specific: what is actually changing?
title: Word to avoid

msg: Avoid "herewith" — just say what you are including
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "instead of" instead of "in lieu of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "under" or "in line with" instead of "pursuant to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "they" instead of "he or she" for gender-neutral language
title: Gender-neutral language

msg: Use "their" instead of "his or her" for gender-neutral language
title: Gender-neutral language

msg: Use "them" instead of "him or her" for gender-neutral language
title: Gender-neutral language

msg: Use "they" instead of "he/she" for gender-neutral language
title: Gender-neutral language

msg: Use "their" instead of "his/her" for gender-neutral language
title: Gender-neutral language

msg: Avoid "simply" — it can make users feel bad if they find it difficult
title: GOV.UK style

msg: GOV.UK style: use "fill in" not "fill out"
title: GOV.UK style

msg: GOV.UK style: do not use FAQs — present information in a user-centred way
title: GOV.UK style

msg: GOV.UK style: use "fine" instead of "financial penalty"
title: GOV.UK style

msg: Use British spelling: "metre" (unit of length). "Meter" is a measuring device.
title: British spelling

msg: Use British spelling: "programme" (unless referring to a computer program)
title: British spelling

msg: Be specific about timing instead of "in due course"
title: Vague timing

msg: Be specific — give a date or say "by [date]"
title: Vague timing

msg: Be specific — give a date or say "by [date]"
title: Vague timing

msg: Be specific about when, or remove if not needed
title: Vague timing

msg: Be specific about timing instead of "shortly"
title: Vague timing

msg: Remove filler — just state the point directly
title: Remove filler

msg: Remove filler — just state the point directly
title: Remove filler

msg: Consider removing — just state what matters
title: Remove filler

msg: Be direct: "you must" or "you need to"
title: Remove filler

msg: Be direct: say who recommends it and what they should do
title: Remove filler

msg: Be direct: "you must" — say who needs to act
title: Remove filler

msg: Be direct: "you need to" or "we need to"
title: Remove filler

msg: Be direct: "you must" or name who needs to act
title: Remove filler

msg: Simplify: "make a payment" → "pay", "make a decision" → "decide", etc.
title: Use plain English

msg: Consider a simpler verb: "carry out an inspection" → "inspect"
title: Use plain English

msg: Simplify: use "consider" instead of "give consideration to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Simplify: use "consider" instead of "take into consideration"
title: Use plain English

msg: Simplify: use "apply" instead of "make an application"
title: Use plain English

msg: Simplify: use "provide for" instead of "make provision for"
title: Use plain English

msg: Simplify: use "decide" instead of "reach a decision"
title: Use plain English

msg: Be specific about what action to take instead of "take steps to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Be specific: "set up", "create", or "introduce" instead of "put in place"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "about" or "for" instead of "in relation to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "to" instead of "for the purpose of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Remove — just say what is being done
title: Remove filler

msg: "Or not" is usually unnecessary — "whether" is enough
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "now" instead of "at this moment in time"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because" instead of "due to the fact that"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "although" instead of "despite the fact that"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because" or "since" instead of "in view of the fact that"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because" instead of "on the grounds that"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "ban", "stop", or "do not allow" instead of "prohibit"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "large", "big", or "a lot of" instead of "substantial"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "next to" or "near" instead of "adjacent to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "for" instead of "in the amount of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "based on" or "because of" instead of "on the basis of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "except" or "apart from" instead of "with the exception of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "without" instead of "in the absence of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "about" or "around" instead of "in the region of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "to" instead of "as a means of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "during" instead of "for the duration of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "after" instead of "at the conclusion of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "can" instead of "has the ability to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "can" instead of "have the ability to"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "until" instead of "until such time as"
title: Use plain English

title: Remove filler

msg: Use "can" instead of "in a position to"

msg: Use "because of" instead of "in light of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "about" or "for" instead of "in connection with"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "fill in" instead of "complete" for forms
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "set up", "find out", or "show" instead of "establish"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "more than" instead of "in excess of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because of" instead of "as a consequence of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because of" instead of "as a result of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "now" or give a specific date instead of "at the time of writing"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "by" or "with" instead of "by means of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "because of" instead of "by virtue of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "mostly" or "mainly" instead of "for the most part"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "during" instead of "in the course of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "first" instead of "in the first instance"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "for" or "to" instead of "in the interests of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "daily" or "every day" instead of "on a daily basis"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "weekly" or "every week" instead of "on a weekly basis"

msg: Use "monthly" or "every month" instead of "on a monthly basis"

msg: Use "yearly" or "every year" instead of "on an annual basis"

msg: Use "that" instead of "to the effect that"

msg: Use "to" instead of "with the aim of"

msg: Remove — just say the thing directly

msg: Use "I have attached" or "here is" instead of "please find attached"

msg: Use "I have included" or "here is" instead of "please find enclosed"

msg: Be direct — say "please" or just ask
title: Use plain English

msg: Name the thing directly instead of saying "the above"
title: Use plain English

title: Remove filler

msg: Use "if you need" instead of "should you require"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "before" instead of "prior to the commencement of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "when we get" or "when you get" instead of "on receipt of"
title: Use plain English

msg: Say what will actually happen, for example "if you do not..."
title: Use plain English

msg: Use "treated as" or "counted as" instead of "deemed to be"
